title: AI in India Today — Healthcare, Agriculture, Vernacular Translation

format_discipline.notes: First non-empty line is H1 title. Sidecar reading.meta.json present and valid. All six fixed sections present in correct order.

length.notes: 1452 words is within 1200–1600. Both WPM formulae place reading within the 6–8 min target.

citations.notes: All three resources cited inline. Bibliography entries [1][2][3] present and match inline markers.

no_forward_references.notes: All cross-references are to prior topics: supervised learning (3.1), LLMs/hallucination (3.2), tokenisation (3.4). All 3.7 introduced concepts are self-contained. No reference to topics beyond 3.7.

faithfulness.notes: All 10 sampled assertions trace directly to corpus sections 5.1–5.4 and section 6–7. Key figures (300M eSanjeevani consultations, 800M internet subscribers, 75% native-language preference, 22 scheduled languages, 11 scripts, 2023–2028 IndiaAI Mission funding window) all confirmed in corpus.

findings.description: Reading does not address the learner as 'you' throughout. Artifact-mix mandate requires active second-person address. All Key Concepts and In Practice paragraphs are written in third person. This is a style drift from the audience mandate.

Several sentences exceed the 25-word target. Notable example at line 17 (telemedicine paragraph): 'AI triage tools are now integrated into eSanjeevani to collect symptoms before the patient reaches a doctor, flag red-alert cases for immediate priority, and pre-fill patient records — reducing the time a doctor spends on history-taking and increasing the number of patients seen per session [1].' (~42 words). Not pervasive but present in 3–4 other sentences.

beginner_readability.notes: Two soft findings (third-person register, occasional long sentences). Threshold for hard-fail escalation is ≥3 distinct findings across checks 5–6 combined. Threshold not met. Terms are defined on first use. Concrete-first ordering maintained throughout. No hedging language detected.

formatting.notes: No paragraph exceeds 6 sentences. Kisan e-Mitra 5-step process rendered as numbered list (lines 25–29). Worked Example rendered as bold-labelled numbered steps (lines 61–78). Table used for multilingual AI challenge comparison (lines 41–45). All introduced terms use bold-term + plain-meaning shape.

assessment_references.notes: No assessment IDs, due weeks, or grade weights found in reading text.

scope.notes: Reading stays within the topic boundary: healthcare AI (computer vision screening, telemedicine), agricultural AI (Kisan e-Mitra, satellite monitoring), and vernacular/multilingual AI (BharatGen, digital divide) in India. All introduced concepts (computer vision, telemedicine, IndiaAI Mission, Kisan e-Mitra, vernacular NLP, multilingual AI, BharatGen, digital divide) are listed in the topic's own artifact_plan. No advanced material imported beyond the depth profile. Prior concepts (supervised learning, LLM, hallucination, tokenisation) cross-referenced with correct prior-topic pointers.

gold_example_comparison.notes: No gold example found at .claude/references/gold-examples/reading/. Evaluation conducted against written standards in artifact-mix.json and reading-critic SKILL.md.

summary: Reading passes all hard checks. Format is clean (no frontmatter, correct six-section structure, valid sidecar). Word count 1452 and estimated read time 7 min are within spec. All three resources cited; bibliography complete. No forward references, no faithfulness failures (10/10 assertions corpus-backed), no scope creep. Two soft findings logged under beginner_readability (third-person register throughout; occasional long sentences) — total 2/3 soft-finding threshold, escalation not triggered. Reading is beginner-usable, well-structured, and appropriately scoped.
